575.content:
[decision] Personal AI Memory Gateway Architecture and Phased Implementation
  Multi-phase architecture using Headroom transport, knowledge-base MCP as canonical source, Claude Mem ingestion, Headroom fallback retrieval, and multi-tool session resumption.
The Personal AI Memory Gateway is a multi-component architecture designed to create persistent, redundant memory continuity across Claude Code, Codex, and optionally Cursor on a single developer machine. The knowledge-base MCP server (Phase 0, audited) is the canonical retrieval source. Headroom (Phase 1) adds transport, compression, and provider cache-control, plus a redundant native memory store that serves as fallback only when the knowledge base's health check confirms failure — never silently on latency or partial errors. Claude Mem (Phase 3) feeds observations one-directionally into the knowledge base via 15-minute polling with SHA256-hash deduplication and last-write-wins conflict resolution. Session-resume capsules (Phase 4) are injected at tool startup, including a `degraded: true` flag when sourced from Headroom's fallback rather than the canonical knowledge base. Phases 5–6 add optional local summarization/classification and curated fine-tuning export, both gated behind stability of earlier phases. Off-machine backup is explicitly deferred to its own phase with its own decision log; same-machine redundancy solves service-crash failure but not machine-loss failure.
  Facts: ["Project uses knowledge-base MCP server (SQLite, entity-observation model, REST API) as single canonical retrieval authority","Headroom acts as transport, provider cache-control, live-zone compression, and redundant native memory store with health-check-gated fallback triggering (decision #10)","Claude Mem integration is read-only and one-directional: new observations polled every 15 minutes, deduped by SHA256 hash, written to knowledge base only; never writes back to Claude Mem (decision #2)","Fallback retrieval from Headroom's native memory only triggers on confirmed knowledge-base health-check failure, never on latency/partial errors; sessions sourced from fallback must be tagged `degraded: true`","Phase 0 completed: audit found 2 entities and 0 observations (not 62 originally claimed); Greptile credential rotated; idle resource baseline ~1.5GB combined","Phase 4 targets Claude Code and Codex via `headroom wrap` wrapper pattern; Cursor (IDE extension) best-effort; if no hook exists, gap documented rather than forced","Same-machine redundancy (Claude Mem + Headroom + knowledge base) addresses service-crash failure only; off-machine backup explicitly deferred to separate phase with own decision log","ingest-claude-mem.ts implements polling job: batches 50, persistent hash-log deduplication, last-write-wins conflict resolution with manual-review flag for confidence delta > 0.3","Phases 5–6 (local quantized model, fine-tuning export) gated behind Phases 0–4 stability verification; Phase 6 requires curated 5,000–20,000 verified examples + manual review"]
  Source: claude-mem #575 (claude, 2026-06-17T12:24:34.727Z)

  MemoryRoutes.ts line 22 defines handleSaveMemory handler for observation persistence
User inspected MemoryRoutes.ts and found the actual write endpoint for observations. The endpoint is POST /api/memory/save, which explains why the earlier HTTP POST probe to /api/observations returned 404. Observations are written via the handleSaveMemory handler in the worker HTTP routes. This endpoint is the mechanism through which new observations are persisted to the claude-mem database.
  Facts: ["Write endpoint: POST /api/memory/save (not POST /api/observations)","Handler registered in MemoryRoutes.ts at line 22","Handler method: this.handleSaveMemory.bind(this)","Earlier 404 on POST /api/observations was expected; correct endpoint uses /api/memory/save path"]

583.content:
[discovery] Claude-mem POST /api/memory/save accepts manual memory payloads
  Handler expects {text, title?, project?} and creates observations with type='discovery'
User inspected the handleSaveMemory handler implementation in MemoryRoutes.ts. The handler accepts manual memory submissions via POST /api/memory/save with a simple payload (text required, title and project optional). It validates the text field, auto-generates a title from the text if not provided, and constructs an observation object with type='discovery'. The observation is persisted to SQLite using the session store, which manages a "manual session" per project. This is the mechanism through which external systems can write observations into claude-mem.
  Facts: ["Expected payload: { text: string, title?: string, project?: string }","text field is required and must be non-empty string","title auto-generated from first 60 chars of text if not provided","Observation type hardcoded to 'discovery' for manual memories","Handler creates or reuses manual session per project via sessionStore.getOrCreateManualSession()","Observation stored to SQLite via sessionStore.storeObservation() with metadata (promptNumber: 0, discoveryTokens: 0)","Response logs observation id, project, and title after successful save"]
  Source: claude-mem #583 (claude, 2026-06-17T16:50:30.401Z)

595.content:
[decision] Phase 5 — Local model integration scoped to non-reasoning tasks
  Wiring Qwen3.5-4B-MLX-4bit for summarization, classification, and redaction assist via OpenAI-compatible endpoint.
Phase 5 accepts a pragmatic model-sizing decision: the brief suggested 7B–8B class models, but the existing 4B Qwen model already loaded in oMLX fits the machine's resource envelope (19.3GB total, 11GB oMLX ceiling, 3.2GB active model) without risking OOM against concurrent Headroom, knowledge-base, and Claude Mem services. The tradeoff — slightly weaker summarization/classification quality — is acceptable because this phase explicitly excludes reasoning-critical work. The implementation stays focused: local model handles three low-stakes assist tasks, accessed via oMLX's OpenAI-compatible API endpoint. Critically, local-model calls bypass Headroom entirely (no provider cost, no cache-control need), keeping Headroom's boundary sharp as the Claude/Codex traffic router. The existing regex-based redaction pass remains the enforcement gate; the local model assists but does not override policy, avoiding a second reasoning path.
  Facts: ["Decision #15: Use already-running Qwen3.5-4B-MLX-4bit (3.2GB active) instead of downloading 7B–8B model; machine has 19.3GB total RAM with 4.3GB free, oMLX ceiling 11GB","Decision #16: Access oMLX via OpenAI-compatible `/v1/chat/completions` endpoint, extending existing knowledge-base/Headroom HTTP client patterns rather than adding new dependency","Decision #17: Local-model calls form distinct code path outside Headroom routing; Headroom stays scoped to Claude/Codex provider traffic only","Phase 5 scope in: summarization, log classification, secret redaction assist; out: coding/architecture/debugging reasoning (stays on Claude/Codex), fine-tuning (Phase 6), new model downloads","Local model used for assist only — existing regex-based redaction gate (Phase 2) remains enforced; local model flags candidates but does not override policy","Acceptance criteria include verifying `/health` and `/v1/chat/completions` with real request/response, wiring at least one call site end-to-end, no change to Headroom scope or regex gate"]
  Source: claude-mem #595 (claude, 2026-06-18T00:38:18.571Z)

598.content:
[discovery] Phase 3 Claude Mem ingestion implementation examined — decision #13 conflict-detection wiring confirmed
  Ingestion script implements hash-based dedup, source-ID conflict detection, and manual-review logging without auto-deactivation.
Phase 3's Claude Mem ingestion is fully implemented and operational. The script polls every 15 minutes (as designed in Decision #5), deduplicates by content hash, and implements the conflict-detection-only model from Decision #13: when the same Claude Mem observation ID reappears with changed content, both previous and incoming versions are logged to manual-review.json for human review, but no observation is auto-deactivated. Content is structured as a multi-line text blob before writing to the knowledge base. This is the wiring point where Phase 5 will inject local-model-assisted summaries: either inline during content construction or as a separate pre-write step before addObservation.
  Facts: ["ingest-claude-mem.ts is a Bun script polling Claude Mem at 127.0.0.1:37701/api/observations in 50-item batches","SHA256 hashing deduplicates observations; processed hashes persisted in ingestion-hashes.json across runs","Source-ID log (ingestion-source-ids.json) tracks last-written hash and content per Claude Mem observation ID for conflict detection","Conflict detection (Decision #13): same source ID with different hash is logged to manual-review.json with previous/incoming content, no auto-resolution","Content construction joins observation fields (type, title, subtitle, narrative, facts, source metadata) with newlines before KB API write","Health checks both Claude Mem and KB REST API (127.0.0.1:3333) before starting ingestion; exits if either unreachable","Each observation written via addObservation(entityName, content) where entityName is project name or default unknown-project"]

599.content:
[discovery] Content construction pattern identified as Phase 5 integration point
  Observation content built as filtered array of fields joined with newlines; summarization can inject into array or post-construct.
The ingestion script constructs observation content as a straightforward array-and-join pattern with no intermediate processing. This creates a clear, low-risk integration point for Phase 5 local-model summarization: either add a summarized_narrative field to the array before joining, or call the local model on the full narrative before including it. The pattern is defensible and requires minimal code change — no refactoring needed, just an additional step in the construction pipeline.
  Facts: ["Content constructed at lines 222–230 as array of optional fields: type+title, subtitle, narrative, facts, source metadata","Array uses .filter(Boolean) to remove null/falsy entries (subtitle, narrative, facts only included if present)","Final .join(\"\\n\") produces multiline string passed to addObservation(entityName, content)","No current summarization or condensation step between observation fetch and content construction","Integration point is either: (1) inject summary field into content array, (2) pre-process narrative before array, or (3) post-process joined string before addObservation"]

600.content:

Phase 5 implementation begins with configuration wiring: the ingestion script now knows how to reach the local oMLX model (127.0.0.1:8005) and which model to use (Qwen3.5-4B-MLX-4bit). The 1500-character threshold is a pragmatic heuristic — narratives shorter than this typically don't benefit from summarization (already concise), while longer ones become candidates for local-model assist. This prepares the ingestion path to call the local model without adding any logic yet; the actual HTTP client and summarization call sites will follow.

The response includes three distinct top-level collections: entities (projects, technologies), observations (memory records), and relations (connections between entities). This confirms the KB is designed to store and serve observations alongside the entity-relation graph, enabling the memory → KB ingestion pipeline to populate observations that can be queried and synthesized.

606.content:
[discovery] Summarization pipeline verified: oMLX reduces long observations while preserving facts
  2225-char test observation summarized to 277 chars; preserved decision details and model names
Testing of the observation summarization feature confirms the Phase 5 integration path works end-to-end. The summarizeIfLong() function successfully calls the oMLX API with a custom prompt that requests preservation of concrete facts (file names, decisions, numbers), applies a 1500-character threshold, and gracefully falls back to original content if the API fails. The test demonstrates a 87.5% reduction in content length while retaining semantic meaning and specific details needed for memory retrieval. This validates that long observations ingested from claude-mem sources will be condensed before storage in the KB, reducing storage overhead while maintaining searchability.
  Facts: ["summarizeIfLong() function tested against oMLX /v1/chat/completions endpoint","1500-character threshold implemented to trigger summarization","Test input: 2225 characters (2000 padding + Phase 5 decision log)","Summarized output: 277 characters (87.5% reduction in length)","Qwen3.5-4B-MLX-4bit model preserved concrete facts: file names (ingest-claude-mem.ts), decision numbers (#15), model details (4B vs 7-8B choice)","Max_tokens set to 200 for summary generation; actual output used ~45 tokens","Fallback pattern implemented: returns original content if API fails or response is empty"]
  Source: claude-mem #606 (claude, 2026-06-18T00:43:26.033Z)

607.content:
[feature] Phase 5: Local model summarization wired into ingestion pipeline (1 of 3 call sites)
  Qwen3.5-4B-MLX integrated via OpenAI-compatible endpoint; summarizes observations >1500 chars before KB write
Phase 5 local model summarization feature completed and deployed. The summarizeIfLong() function integrates Qwen3.5-4B-MLX running on oMLX (127.0.0.1:8005) to condense long observations before writing to the knowledge base. The implementation uses the OpenAI-compatible /v1/chat/completions endpoint, preserving raw content for hashing/conflict-detection while only summarizing the KB-written copy. Threshold set to 1500 characters; below that observations pass through unchanged. The feature gracefully degrades if oMLX is unreachable or errors, returning original content. Verified end-to-end with live ingestion (13 observations written) and isolated test with synthetic 2225-char input reduced to 277 chars while preserving semantic meaning. Two additional call sites (log classification, secret-redaction-assist) remain unbuilt but documented for future Phase 5 work. No changes to existing Headroom routing or redaction gate.
  Facts: ["Decision #15: Used already-running oMLX (127.0.0.1:8005, Qwen3.5-4B-MLX-4bit) instead of pulling 7B-8B model due to RAM constraints (4.3GB free, oMLX ceiling 11GB)","Decision #16: Accessed oMLX via OpenAI-compatible /v1/chat/completions endpoint directly; no new client dependency","Decision #17: Local model calls on separate code path, not routed through Headroom; Headroom remains scoped to Claude/Codex provider traffic per Phase 1","summarizeIfLong() function implemented with SUMMARIZE_THRESHOLD_CHARS = 1500 constant","Summarization wired after conflict-hash computation, before addObservation() call; raw content preserved for hashing/conflict-detection","Live ingestion run: 13 observations written, all under threshold, correctly passed through unsummarized","Test with 2225-char synthetic input: summarized to 277 chars (87.5% reduction), concrete facts preserved (Phase 5, decision #15, 4B model choice)","Fails open on any oMLX error/unreachability; returns original content instead of blocking observation write","Log classification and secret-redaction-assist call sites deferred; minimum one of three call sites satisfied per spec"]
  Source: claude-mem #607 (claude, 2026-06-18T00:44:25.759Z)

608.content:

  session-state-2026-06-18.md documents Personal AI Memory Gateway architecture, 4 decisions, research on 5 AI tools
Session documentation created during Phase 5 work captures comprehensive project state and architecture. The session-state-2026-06-18.md file documents the Personal AI Memory Gateway intent (multi-AI session continuity), four key architectural decisions (KB canonical authority, degraded flag handling, conflict detection, conflict resolution), and extensive research findings on five AI tools (Claude Code, Codex, Cursor, Gemini, Antigravity). Research surfaced the 60K token startup problem caused by overlapping context injection systems: claude-mem plugin cache (601MB), 9 active plugins loading their own CLAUDE.mds, and GitKraken hooks firing on every interaction. The headroom-ai-memory folder appears to be project scaffolding/documentation for the Headroom component. These artifacts represent durable knowledge capture of the system architecture and integration points.

612.content:
[decision] Phase 5: Local Quantized Model Architecture for Non-Reasoning Tasks
  oMLX 4B model planned for summarization, log classification, secret-redaction assist without Headroom routing.
Phase 5 defines a focused strategy to use an already-running local quantized model (oMLX with Qwen3.5-4B-MLX-4bit) for non-reasoning tasks that don't need frontier-model reasoning, enabling zero API cost for summarization, log classification, and secret-redaction assist. The 4B model was chosen despite the brief suggesting 7B-8B, because the machine has memory constraints (19.3GB total, ~4.3GB free) and oMLX reports an 11GB ceiling for larger models; 4B is acceptable since Phase 5 explicitly excludes reasoning-critical work. Local-model calls are routed via a separate code path independent of Headroom (the Claude/Codex provider gateway from Phase 1), preserving architectural boundaries. Access uses OpenAI-compatible /v1/chat/completions without a new dependency. The local model is used only for assist (suggestions), never enforcement — the existing regex-based secret redaction remains the final gate before writing. If oMLX is unavailable, a health check causes the local-model step to be skipped without blocking the primary Claude/Codex path. Three risks were identified: oMLX downtime (mitigated by health-check fail-closed), hallucination (mitigated by assist-only use), and RAM pressure (monitored, not currently critical).
  Facts: ["Phase 5 specification approved 2026-06-18 for integrating local quantized model into personal AI workflow","oMLX server (127.0.0.1:8005) with Qwen3.5-4B-MLX-4bit model already running; scope excludes managing oMLX lifecycle","4B model chosen over 7B-8B due to machine RAM constraints (19.3GB total, ~4.3GB free, oMLX reports 11GB ceiling for larger models)","Local model calls routed via separate code path, never through Headroom (which remains scoped to Claude/Codex provider traffic only)","Three call sites planned: text summarization, log line classification, secret redaction candidate suggestion","Local model used for assist only; existing regex-based secret redaction remains the enforcement gate","oMLX accessed via OpenAI-compatible /v1/chat/completions endpoint; no new client library dependency","Health-check on /health endpoint required before use; fails closed if oMLX unavailable (doesn't block primary Claude/Codex path)","Acceptance criteria: verify oMLX /health and /v1/chat/completions, wire one call site end-to-end, update progress.md"]
  Source: claude-mem #612 (claude, 2026-06-18T00:53:16.687Z)

613.content:
[discovery] Phase 5 Summarization Already Wired in Claude Mem Ingestion
  ingest-claude-mem.ts calls oMLX for content >1500 chars; redaction enforcement must be verified in SDK layer.
Phase 5 summarization wiring is already implemented in the Claude Mem ingestion pipeline (ingest-claude-mem.ts), not a greenfield task. Content over 1500 characters is sent to the running oMLX server (Qwen3.5-4B-MLX-4bit at 127.0.0.1:8005) with a prompt requesting concrete-facts summaries in 2-3 sentences. The summarized (or original, on error) content is then posted to the knowledge-base REST API's POST /observations route. The knowledge-base has comprehensive regex-based redaction (redact.ts) covering SSH keys, DB passwords, API keys, Bearer tokens, and environment-variable secrets, and it is applied in graph.ts before database write. However, the redaction enforcement point for summarized content passing through the POST /observations REST endpoint requires verification — the SDK's addObservation() method either applies redaction before persistence, or the REST route handler does so before calling the SDK. The existing redaction gate (regex patterns in redact.ts) remains the enforced control point for all sensitive-data exclusion before any write to the observations table.
  Facts: ["ingest-claude-mem.ts already implements summarizeIfLong() calling OMLX at 127.0.0.1:8005/v1/chat/completions with Qwen3.5-4B-MLX-4bit","Summarization threshold set to 1500 chars; prompt asks for 2-3 sentence summary with concrete facts (files, decisions, numbers)","summarizeIfLong() max_tokens set to 200; returns original content on oMLX error or non-2xx response (fail-safe)","Ingestion chain: summarized = await summarizeIfLong(content), then written = await addObservation(entityName, summarized) to REST API POST /observations","Knowledge-base redact.ts applies regex enforcement for SSH keys, DB passwords, API keys, Bearer tokens, env vars, labeled secrets before DB write","graph.ts imports and applies redaction via redactObject() on full event and individual field redaction (files_touched, commands_run, repo_path, narrative)","REST API POST /observations route exists but redaction application point in SDK.addObservation() not yet verified in grep results"]
  Source: claude-mem #613 (claude, 2026-06-18T00:53:35.241Z)
